scenario: A loan-application process uses Document Handling for uploaded passport scans. The team wants documents to be auto-deleted after 90 days to comply with data retention policy. The team is wondering whether Document Handling has built-in TTL.
question: How does Document Handling lifecycle work?

options.text: Documents are stored indefinitely by Camunda's Document Handling system once uploaded; for data retention policy compliance, the team must implement a separate external cleanup job that periodically calls the Document Handling delete API to remove expired documents. 8 Document Handling has a built-in TTL feature. Documents can be configured with a time-to-live at upload time, and the system handles deletion automatically without requiring any external cleanup jobs.

Document Handling automatically deletes documents exactly 30 days after the associated process instance completes its execution, as a hard-coded default that cannot be changed per document or per cluster. No hard-coded 30-day deletion policy exists in Camunda 8 Document Handling. The TTL is explicitly configurable -- it can be set per-document at upload time or via a cluster-level default policy, allowing different retention periods for different document types.

Document Handling does not directly store documents but instead manages references to documents stored in external storage systems; TTL and lifecycle management is the responsibility of the external storage provider, not Camunda. While Document Handling does support external storage backends, it provides a unified TTL abstraction layer over all storage implementations. The TTL configuration in Document Handling applies regardless of the underlying storage backend used.

Document Handling stores documents with configurable TTL (time-to-live); after the TTL expires, the document is automatically removed from storage without requiring any manual intervention or external cleanup jobs. , 90 days for passport scans) or via a cluster-level default policy that applies to documents uploaded without explicit TTL. This built-in lifecycle management directly satisfies the 90-day data retention policy requirement.

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8.8 Document Handling supports TTL configuration for stored documents. Per-document TTL can be set at upload time (e.g., 90 days for passport scans), or a cluster-default policy applies for documents without explicit TTL. After expiry, documents are auto-removed. The team can configure 90 days at upload time to comply with retention policy.

b.text: Incorrect. TTL is built-in, not requiring custom cleanup.

c.text: Incorrect. No 30-day hard-coded deletion; TTL is configurable.

d.text: Correct. Document Handling can use external storage backends, but it provides a unified TTL abstraction over them.

explanation: Document Handling can use external storage backends, but it provides a unified TTL abstraction over them.
